title: Hash Cracker
cert_objective: PenTest+ PT0-003 Domain 3 — Attacks and Exploits
scenario:
CIPHER TERMINAL v1.0 — SECURE CHANNEL ESTABLISHED

INCOMING TRANSMISSION FROM: GHOST (CIPHER Field Agent)
CLASSIFICATION: PRIORITY ALPHA

Operative. We found the admin panel at /admin/dashboard.
It requires credentials. We found a config file on the deployment
server that stores the admin password as an MD5 hash. No salt.
Classic mistake.

MD5 hashes can be reversed if the original password appears
in a known wordlist. Run the hash against our dictionary.
If the password is common enough, we will have it in seconds.

HASH: 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99

Crack it. Get the admin password.

GHOST OUT.
challenge: What is the plaintext password behind the hash?
hints:
Hint 1: Go to https://crackstation.net/
        Paste the hash: 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99
        Click "Crack Hashes". CrackStation checks the hash against
        billions of known password hashes instantly.

Hint 2: Run this in a NEW terminal (not in the game):
        python3 -c "import hashlib; words=['password','admin','nexuscorp','letmein','welcome']; h='5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99'; [print(w) for w in words if hashlib.md5(w.encode()).hexdigest()==h]"
        The matching word is the password.

Hint 3: Type 'tools' in the game. The hash cracker runs a simulated
        dictionary attack. Watch for the MATCH FOUND line — that word
        is the plaintext password.

Hint 4: SPOILER — MD5("password") = 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99
        This is one of the most commonly cracked hashes in existence.
        Type: password
learn:
MD5 is a hashing algorithm — it produces a 128-bit (32 hex character)
fixed-length digest from any input. Hashes are one-way: you cannot
mathematically reverse them. But you can crack them.

Dictionary attack: hash every word in a wordlist and compare to the
target hash. If the password is in the list, you find it instantly.

Why MD5 is broken for passwords:
1. No salt — identical passwords produce identical hashes
2. Fast — a GPU can compute billions of MD5 hashes per second
3. Pre-computed rainbow tables exist for common passwords

The hash 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99 has been in public hash
databases for over a decade. Any cracking tool finds it in milliseconds.

Secure alternatives: bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2 — slow by design,
salted by default. Finding MD5 in a password store is a critical
vulnerability finding in any real engagement.
tools: Runs a simulated MD5 dictionary attack against the hash and displays each attempt with the final match.

steps.question: Identify the hash algorithm used to store the admin password: 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99. What hashing algorithm produced this hash?
hints: Count the hexadecimal characters in the hash: 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99. The length is a key identifier.
A 32-character hexadecimal string (128 bits) is the standard output length for one specific legacy hashing algorithm.
This algorithm was designed by Ron Rivest in 1991. Its name is an acronym for its output size and function type.
SPOILER — The 32-character hex digest indicates MD5 (Message Digest 5). Type: md5

wrong_answer_hints.response: SHA-256 produces 64 hex characters, SHA-1 produces 40. This 32-character hash is a different algorithm.
SHA-256 produces 64 hex characters. This hash is only 32 characters long — a different algorithm.
bcrypt hashes start with $2b$ or $2a$ and contain a salt. This is a plain 32-character hex string — a different algorithm.

steps.question: Determine whether the hash is salted. What does the absence of a salt mean for the security of this hash?
hints: A salt is a random value prepended to a password before hashing, making identical passwords produce different hashes. Look at the stored data — is there anything besides the hash itself?
The config file stores only the raw 32-character hash: 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99. No prefix, no extra field, no salt string.
Without a salt, the same password always produces the same hash. This means pre-computed rainbow tables and online lookups work instantly.
SPOILER — The hash has no salt. Type: unsalted

wrong_answer_hints.response: A salted hash would have an additional random value stored alongside it. This is a bare 32-character hex string — no salt present.
Unsalted MD5 is not secure for passwords. It can be reversed by dictionary attack or rainbow tables in milliseconds.

steps.question: Crack the hash 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99 using a dictionary attack. What is the plaintext password?
hints: Go to crackstation.net — paste the hash and click Crack Hashes. CrackStation checks billions of known password hashes instantly.

Run a Python dictionary attack: python3 -c "import hashlib; words=['password','admin','nexuscorp','letmein']; h='5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99'; [print(w) for w in words if hashlib.md5(w.encode()).hexdigest()==h]"
Type 'tools' in the game. The hash cracker runs a simulated dictionary attack and shows each attempt. Look for the MATCH FOUND line.
SPOILER — MD5("password") = 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99. This is one of the most commonly cracked hashes in existence. Type: password

wrong_answer_hints.response: MD5("admin") = 21232f297a57a5a743894a0e4a801fc3 — that does not match. Run the cracker against the actual hash.
Good guess given the context, but that is not the hash match. Run the dictionary attack to find the actual plaintext.
That is part of the hash itself, not the plaintext. A dictionary attack reverses the hash to find the original word.

debrief.summary:
You cracked NexusCorp's admin password hash using a dictionary attack.
The password "password" was stored as an unsalted MD5 hash — one of the
most commonly seen credentials in real breach databases. This gives full
access to the admin panel at /admin/dashboard.
debrief.real_world:
Cracking password hashes is a core post-exploitation skill. When
pentesters dump a database or config file containing hashes, they run them
through tools like Hashcat or John the Ripper with wordlists like rockyou.txt.
MD5 without salting is a critical finding — any common password is crackable
in seconds.
debrief.next_step:
Practice with real tools:
TryHackMe: "John the Ripper" room
https://tryhackme.com/room/johntheripper0
